Party_Details.Name: Chan Tong Fan

Facts.Text: Chan Tong Fan is the second child of Mr Chiam Heng Luan and Mdm Lim Wee Leng, who set up Chiam Heng Luan Realty Pte Ltd and Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd in 1971.
For the financial year 2007, Chan Tong Fan received dividends of $168,000 from Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.
For the financial year 2008, Chan Tong Fan received dividends of $168,000 from Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.
For the financial year 2009, Chan Tong Fan received dividends of $168,000 from Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a minority shareholder should be granted leave under Section 216A of the Companies Act to commence a statutory derivative action on behalf of the company against its directors for alleged breaches of duty relating to excessive remuneration, property mismanagement, unaccounted renovation expenses, and foreign exchange losses.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Section 216A of the Companies Act, a complainant seeking to bring a derivative action must demonstrate that: (1) 14 days' notice has been given to the directors; (2) the application is made in good faith; and (3) it appears prima facie to be in the interests of the company that the action be brought. The court will not grant leave if the proposed claims lack a reasonable legal or factual basis, or if the impugned acts involve genuine commercial judgments within the directors' purview that have been, or could validly be, ratified by an independent majority of shareholders.
Party_Details.Application: Chan Tong Fan, a minority shareholder holding 15.56% of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd, sought to sue his siblings (the current directors) on behalf of the company. He alleged four main breaches of duty: (a) that the directors paid themselves excessive and unapproved remuneration, relying heavily on a spike in the 2009 accounts; (b) that they breached their duties by leaving adjacent land vacant since 1993, incurring property taxes without generating income; (c) that they could not produce invoices for approximately $800,000 in renovation expenses spanning over a decade; and (d) that they negligently maintained foreign currency deposits overseas, resulting in over $1 million in unrealized exchange losses. The legal tension centers on whether these allegations represent genuine, actionable breaches of fiduciary duty that the company ought to pursue, or merely retrospective disagreements over commercial decisions and administrative errors driven by an underlying family dispute.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that the plaintiff failed to establish that the proposed action was prima facie in the interests of the company, and further found a lack of good faith. Regarding remuneration, the court accepted evidence that the spike in the 2009 accounts was a typographical error grouping staff salaries with directors' pay; furthermore, the directors' salaries had been routinely approved by shareholders at AGMs for decades. The non-development of the adjacent land was a commercial judgment hindered by zoning restrictions, a situation the founders themselves had accepted during their lifetimes. The renovation expenses, averaging about $55,000 annually for an aging hotel, were deemed entirely reasonable, and the lack of comprehensive historical invoices did not equate to misappropriation. Similarly, the maintenance of overseas deposits and the resulting unrealized exchange losses were matters of commercial discretion regarding currency risk, not breaches of fiduciary duty. Given that the majority of independent shareholders had explicitly voted against suing the directors and ratified their conduct, the court concluded the plaintiff's claims were frivolous, lacked reasonable grounds, and were pursued with a determination to find fault rather than to genuinely protect the company's interests. The application for leave was dismissed.

Party_Details.Name: Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd was set up in 1971 by Mr Chiam Heng Luan and Mdm Lim Wee Leng to take over the family business of the Sloane Court Hotel carried on at 17 Balmoral Road.

In 1971, when Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd and Chiam Heng Luan Realty Pte Ltd were incorporated, ownership of the Balmoral Property at 17 Balmoral Road was transferred to Chiam Heng Luan Realty Pte Ltd, and the hotel business was transferred to Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd, which became tenant of the Balmoral Property.
As at October 2012, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd had a paid-up capital of $270,002 comprising 270,002 ordinary shares of $1.00 each.
In October 2012, the shareholders of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd comprised Chan Tong Fan, all his siblings, and the estates of their parents, with Chan Tong Fan holding 15.56% of the shares.
When Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1971, Mr Chiam Heng Luan and his wife Mdm Lim Wee Leng were the sole directors and shareholders.
In 1988, Ms Chiam Ai Thong and Mr Chiam Toon Chew were appointed directors of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd by Mr Chiam Heng Luan.
In 1989, Mr Chiam Toon Tau was appointed a director of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.

At a board meeting of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd in April 1990, Mr Chiam Toon Tau was appointed managing director, Ms Chiam Ai Thong was appointed acting director, and Mr Chiam Toon Chew was appointed working director, and their salaries were approved at that meeting.
By October 2012, three of Chan Tong Fan’s siblings were directors of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd and together held 37.42% of its shares.
As at the commencement of Originating Summons No 935 of 2012, the main business of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd was operating the Sloane Court Hotel and a restaurant on the Balmoral Property.
Minute sheets and attendance lists show annual general meetings were held in the years 1974 to 1978.
Minute sheets and attendance lists show annual general meetings were held in the years 1980 to 1989.
Minute sheets and attendance lists show annual general meetings were held in the years 1991 to 1998.
Minute sheets and attendance lists show annual general meetings were held in the years 2000 to 2011.
In 1992, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd requested permission to develop the SCH land into service apartments.
In 1994, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd had plans to build an addition to the hotel on the SCH land.
In February 1996, an extraordinary general meeting was held to discuss the use of the SCH land, but no conclusion on its use was reached.
The SCH land comprised two plots adjacent to the Balmoral Property and was zoned for different uses, commercial and residential, so it could not be amalgamated into one plot for one use.
In early 1993, a two-storey block of flats on the SCH land was demolished, and the site was left vacant thereafter.
From 1993 onwards, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd incurred annual property tax on the SCH land, which by 2009 amounted to approximately $26,000 per year.
For the financial year 2008, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd’s accounts recorded $142,849 as remuneration paid to the directors.

For the financial year 2009, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd’s accounts initially recorded $221,189 as remuneration for directors, a figure that included salaries of other employees due to a typographical error, while the actual remuneration paid to the directors was stated to be $87,100.

Between the financial years 1995 and 1996, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd recorded expenditures described as renovation, furniture and fittings, electrical equipment, and air-conditioning in its accounts as part of a total of approximately $804,467.92 spent over specified years.

Between the financial years 1999 and 2010, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd recorded expenditures described as renovation, furniture and fittings, electrical equipment, and air-conditioning in its accounts, contributing to a total of approximately $804,467.92 over the identified periods.
For the year 2000, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd’s accounts recorded renovation expenses of $240,177.

From the financial year ending 31 December 2003 to the financial year ending 31 December 2010, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd’s accounts reflected unrealised foreign exchange differences on overseas investments and deposits, which the plaintiff aggregated as $1,082,445 in unrealised exchange losses.

During the period from the financial year ending 31 December 2003 to the financial year ending 31 December 2010, Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd maintained bank accounts and investments in the United Kingdom, Hong Kong SAR, and Malaysia, resulting in foreign currency translation effects in its financial statements.

At the 2011 annual general meeting of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd, the directors explained that the 2009 accounts contained a typographical error in the directors’ remuneration figure and clarified the actual amount paid to the directors.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company, acting through its majority shareholders, can validly decline to pursue litigation against its directors for alleged historical mismanagement, and whether a minority shareholder can force the company to litigate via a statutory derivative action.
Party_Details.Rule: The decision to commence litigation on behalf of a company lies with its board of directors and, ultimately, its shareholders. Under Section 216A of the Companies Act, a court will only override the corporate majority and permit a minority shareholder to sue in the company's name if the proposed claims are prima facie in the company's best interests. A derivative action will not be permitted if the alleged wrongs involve rational commercial judgments that have been fully disclosed to and validly ratified by an independent majority of the shareholders.
Party_Details.Application: Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd is a family-owned company operating a hotel. A minority shareholder sought to force the company to sue three of its directors (his siblings) for alleged breaches of duty. The allegations centered on past remuneration approvals, a decades-long decision to leave adjacent land undeveloped due to zoning issues, an accumulation of renovation expenses, and unrealized foreign exchange losses from holding overseas deposits. After the plaintiff raised these issues, the company convened an extraordinary general meeting and an annual general meeting in late 2012. At these meetings, the majority of shareholders reviewed the allegations, accepted the directors' explanations (including that a spike in 2009 remuneration was a mere typographical error), explicitly voted against suing the directors, and ratified their conduct. The legal inquiry assesses whether the company, having ratified the directors' conduct, should nonetheless be compelled by the court to serve as the nominal plaintiff in a derivative action.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court respected the doctrine of majority rule and corporate autonomy. It found that the shareholders of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd had been fully informed of the plaintiff's allegations and had made a rational, collective decision not to pursue litigation against the directors. The court scrutinized the underlying claims and agreed with the majority shareholders that the claims lacked merit: the remuneration was historically approved and earned; the non-development of the land and the handling of foreign deposits were protected exercises of commercial judgment; and the renovation expenses were necessary and reasonable for an aging hotel. Because the directors' actions were either lawful commercial decisions or validly ratified by the shareholders without any evidence of fraud or oppression of the minority, the court determined that forcing the company into litigation was entirely contrary to its interests. The application to use the company's name for a derivative action was therefore dismissed.

Party_Details.Name: Chiam Toon Tau and another, non-parties

Facts.Text: In 1988, Ms Chiam Ai Thong and Mr Chiam Toon Chew were appointed directors of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd by Mr Chiam Heng Luan.
In 1989, Mr Chiam Toon Tau was appointed a director of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.

At a board meeting of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd in April 1990, Mr Chiam Toon Tau was appointed managing director, Ms Chiam Ai Thong was appointed acting director, and Mr Chiam Toon Chew was appointed working director, and their salaries were approved at that meeting.

As at October 2012, Ms Chiam Ai Thong, Mr Chiam Toon Chew, and Mr Chiam Toon Tau were directors of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd and were identified as the prospective defendants to the action that Chan Tong Fan sought leave to commence.
For the financial years 2007 to 2009, Mr Chiam Toon Tau received dividends of $168,000 per year from Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.
For the financial years 2007 to 2009, Ms Chiam Ai Thong received dividends of $56,000 per year from Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.
For the financial years 2007 to 2009, Mr Chiam Toon Chew received dividends of $180,000 per year from Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether directors of a family-owned company can be subjected to a statutory derivative action for historical decisions regarding remuneration, property development, operational expenses, and foreign currency management, or whether such decisions are protected exercises of commercial judgment ratified by the shareholders.
Party_Details.Rule: Courts will not grant leave for a derivative action against directors if the proposed claims challenge bona fide commercial decisions that fall within the reasonable scope of the board's authority. Furthermore, if an independent majority of shareholders, fully informed of the facts, validly ratifies the directors' conduct or votes against initiating litigation, the court will generally view the proposed action as contrary to the company's best interests and deny leave to sue.
Party_Details.Application: The non-party interveners (Chiam Toon Tau, Chiam Toon Chew, and Chiam Ai Thong) were long-serving directors of Sloane Court Hotel Pte Ltd, having been appointed by their parents in the late 1980s. A minority shareholder (their sibling) sought court permission to sue them on behalf of the company, alleging they paid themselves excessive unapproved remuneration, negligently left corporate land undeveloped since 1993, incurred undocumented renovation expenses, and mismanaged foreign currency deposits resulting in unrealized losses. The directors intervened in the application to present corporate records demonstrating that their salaries had been formally approved by the board and shareholders for decades, that the land remained undeveloped due to intractable zoning restrictions, that the renovation expenses were necessary for the hotel's upkeep, and that the foreign currency strategy was a deliberate commercial decision. Crucially, in late 2012, the majority of the company's shareholders explicitly voted to ratify the directors' actions and rejected the proposal to sue them.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court scrutinized the proposed claims against the directors and found them entirely devoid of merit. The directors' salaries had been consistently approved at AGMs since 1990, and the seemingly massive spike in 2009 was proven to be a mere typographical error in the accounts. The decisions to leave the adjacent land undeveloped and to maintain overseas currency deposits were clear exercises of commercial judgment, which the court will not second-guess with the benefit of hindsight. The renovation expenses were deemed reasonable and necessary for an aging property, and the lack of pristine archival documentation did not indicate fraud. Most importantly, the court gave heavy weight to the fact that the majority of shareholders had reviewed these exact allegations and formally voted to ratify the directors' conduct. Concluding that the claims were frivolous, lacked any reasonable legal basis, and were pursued in bad faith by a disgruntled sibling, the court dismissed the application, protecting the directors from the derivative suit.
